title: Chapter 32: SQL Exercise Set C - Part 1 (Questions 1–8)

lessons.title: Reformat Department Table
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to reformat the `Department` table such that there is a department `id` column and a revenue column for each month.

Return the result table in any order.
lessons.expectedQuery:
SELECT id,
       SUM(CASE WHEN month = 'Jan' THEN revenue ELSE NULL END) AS Jan_Revenue,
       SUM(CASE WHEN month = 'Feb' THEN revenue ELSE NULL END) AS Feb_Revenue,
       SUM(CASE WHEN month = 'Mar' THEN revenue ELSE NULL END) AS Mar_Revenue,
       SUM(CASE WHEN month = 'Apr' THEN revenue ELSE NULL END) AS Apr_Revenue,
       SUM(CASE WHEN month = 'May' THEN revenue ELSE NULL END) AS May_Revenue,
       SUM(CASE WHEN month = 'Jun' THEN revenue ELSE NULL END) AS Jun_Revenue,
       SUM(CASE WHEN month = 'Jul' THEN revenue ELSE NULL END) AS Jul_Revenue,
       SUM(CASE WHEN month = 'Aug' THEN revenue ELSE NULL END) AS Aug_Revenue,
       SUM(CASE WHEN month = 'Sep' THEN revenue ELSE NULL END) AS Sep_Revenue,
       SUM(CASE WHEN month = 'Oct' THEN revenue ELSE NULL END) AS Oct_Revenue,
       SUM(CASE WHEN month = 'Nov' THEN revenue ELSE NULL END) AS Nov_Revenue,
       SUM(CASE WHEN month = 'Dec' THEN revenue ELSE NULL END) AS Dec_Revenue
FROM Department
GROUP BY id;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Write your SELECT query below
SELECT id,
       SUM(CASE WHEN month = 'Jan' THEN revenue ELSE NULL END) AS Jan_Revenue,
       SUM(CASE WHEN month = 'Feb' THEN revenue ELSE NULL END) AS Feb_Revenue
       -- Add other months here...
FROM Department
GROUP BY id;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Groups the department records by id and pivots the monthly revenue values using conditional aggregation with SUM and CASE WHEN.

lessons.title: Patients With a Condition
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to find the patient_id, patient_name, and conditions of the patients who have Type I Diabetes. Type I Diabetes always starts with `DIAB1` prefix.

Return the result table in any order.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT patient_id, patient_name, conditions FROM Patients WHERE conditions LIKE 'DIAB1%' OR conditions LIKE '% DIAB1%';
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Write your SELECT query below
SELECT patient_id, patient_name, conditions
FROM Patients
WHERE ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Checks if 'DIAB1' appears either at the very beginning of the conditions string (LIKE 'DIAB1%') or as a later word preceded by a space (LIKE '% DIAB1%').

lessons.title: Rearrange Products Table
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to rearrange the `Products` table so that each row has `(product_id, store, price)`. If a product is not available in a store, do not include a row with that `product_id` and `store` combination in the result table.

Return the result table in any order.
lessons.expectedQuery:
SELECT product_id, 'store1' AS store, store1 AS price FROM Products WHERE store1 IS NOT NULL
UNION
SELECT product_id, 'store2' AS store, store2 AS price FROM Products WHERE store2 IS NOT NULL
UNION
SELECT product_id, 'store3' AS store, store3 AS price FROM Products WHERE store3 IS NOT NULL;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Write your SELECT query below
SELECT product_id, 'store1' AS store, store1 AS price 
FROM Products 
WHERE store1 IS NOT NULL
UNION
-- Write queries for store2 and store3
;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Unpivots columns to rows by selecting product_id, a constant string for the store name, and the store price for each store column that is not null, combining the results using UNION.

lessons.title: Daily Leads and Partners
lessons.description:
For each `date_id` and `make_name`, find the number of distinct `lead_id`'s and distinct `partner_id`'s.

Return the result table in any order.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT date_id, make_name, COUNT(DISTINCT lead_id) AS unique_leads, COUNT(DISTINCT partner_id) AS unique_partners FROM DailySales GROUP BY date_id, make_name;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Write your SELECT query below
SELECT date_id, make_name, COUNT(DISTINCT lead_id) AS unique_leads
FROM DailySales
GROUP BY ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Groups the sales records by date_id and make_name, then aggregates counts of unique leads and unique partners using COUNT(DISTINCT ...).

lessons.title: Employees Earning More Than Their Managers
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to find the employees who earn more than their managers.

Return the result table in any order.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT e1.name AS Employee FROM Employee AS e1 INNER JOIN Employee AS e2 ON e1.managerId = e2.id WHERE e1.salary > e2.salary;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Write your SELECT query below
SELECT e1.name AS Employee
FROM Employee AS e1
INNER JOIN Employee AS e2 ON 
WHERE ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Performs a self-join comparing employee salary with manager salary, filtering where employee salary exceeds manager salary.

lessons.title: Find Total Time Spent by Each Employee
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to calculate the total time in minutes spent by each employee on each day at the office. Note that within one day, an employee can enter and leave more than once. The time spent in the office for a single entry is `out_time - in_time`.

Return the result table in any order.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT event_day AS day, emp_id, (SUM(out_time) - SUM(in_time)) AS total_time FROM Employees GROUP BY event_day, emp_id;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Write your SELECT query below
SELECT event_day AS day, emp_id, 
FROM Employees
GROUP BY ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Groups the events by day and employee ID, summing the differences between out_time and in_time for each day.

lessons.title: Swap Salary
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to swap all `'f'` and `'m'` values (i.e., change all `'f'` values to `'m'` and vice versa) with a single update statement and no intermediate temporary tables.

Note that you must write a single update statement, and end it with a query to fetch the updated table.
lessons.expectedQuery: UPDATE Salary SET sex = CASE WHEN sex = 'm' THEN 'f' WHEN sex = 'f' THEN 'm' END; SELECT * FROM Salary;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Updates the gender column in-place by checking if sex equals 'm' (changing it to 'f') or 'f' (changing it to 'm') using a CASE expression.

lessons.title: Duplicate Emails
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to report all the duplicate emails. Note that it is guaranteed that the email field is not NULL.

Return the result table in any order.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT email FROM Person GROUP BY email HAVING COUNT(email) > 1;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Write your SELECT query below
SELECT email 
FROM Person 
GROUP BY email 
HAVING ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Groups rows by email address and filters for email groups that have a count of elements greater than 1.

title: Chapter 33: SQL Exercise Set C - Part 2 (Questions 9–16)

lessons.title: Group Sold Products By The Date
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to find for each date the number of different products sold and their names. The sold products names for each date should be sorted lexicographically.

Return the result table ordered by `sell_date`.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT sell_date, COUNT(DISTINCT product) AS num_sold, group_concat(DISTINCT product) AS products FROM (SELECT DISTINCT sell_date, product FROM Activities ORDER BY sell_date, product) GROUP BY sell_date ORDER BY sell_date;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Write your SELECT query below
-- (Tip: Since SQLite doesn't support ORDER BY inside group_concat, sort the distinct pairs in a subquery first)
SELECT sell_date, COUNT(DISTINCT product) AS num_sold, group_concat(DISTINCT product) AS products
FROM (
    SELECT DISTINCT sell_date, product 
    FROM Activities 
    ORDER BY sell_date, product
)
GROUP BY sell_date
ORDER BY sell_date;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Since SQLite does not support custom sort order in group_concat, we first query unique sell_date and product combinations ordered lexicographically in a subquery, then aggregate them using group_concat in the outer query.

lessons.title: The Latest Login in 2020
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to report the latest login for all users in the year 2020. Do not include the users who did not login in 2020.

Return the result table in any order.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT user_id, MAX(time_stamp) AS last_stamp FROM Logins WHERE strftime('%Y', time_stamp) = '2020' GROUP BY user_id;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Write your SELECT query below
SELECT user_id, MAX(time_stamp) AS last_stamp
FROM Logins
WHERE 
GROUP BY user_id;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Filters for login logs occurring in the year 2020 using the SQLite strftime('%Y', time_stamp) = '2020' expression, grouping by user_id to extract their maximum (latest) timestamp.

lessons.title: Not Boring Movies
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to report the movies with an odd-numbered ID and a description that is not "boring".

Return the result table ordered by rating in descending order.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT id, movie, description, rating FROM Cinema WHERE id % 2 = 1 AND description != 'boring' ORDER BY rating DESC;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Write your SELECT query below
SELECT id, movie, description, rating
FROM Cinema
WHERE 
ORDER BY ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Retrieves movie details for records with odd IDs (id % 2 = 1) and with description values other than 'boring', sorted by rating descending.

lessons.title: Employees With Missing Information
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to report the IDs of all the employees with missing information. The information of an employee is missing if the employee's name is missing, or the employee's salary is missing.

Return the result table ordered by `employee_id` in ascending order.
lessons.expectedQuery:
SELECT employee_id FROM Employees WHERE employee_id NOT IN (SELECT employee_id FROM Salaries)
UNION
SELECT employee_id FROM Salaries WHERE employee_id NOT IN (SELECT employee_id FROM Employees)
ORDER BY employee_id;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Write your SELECT query below
-- (Tip: Since SQLite doesn't support FULL OUTER JOIN directly, query missing employee ids using UNION)
SELECT employee_id FROM Employees WHERE employee_id NOT IN (SELECT employee_id FROM Salaries)
UNION
;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Uses UNION to combine employee IDs that exist in the Employees table but are missing from Salaries with employee IDs that exist in the Salaries table but are missing from Employees, ordered in ascending order.

lessons.title: Delete Duplicate Emails
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to delete all duplicate emails, keeping only one unique email with the smallest `id`.

Note that you must write a DELETE statement, and end it with a query to fetch the updated table.
lessons.expectedQuery: DELETE FROM Person WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT MIN(id) FROM Person GROUP BY email); SELECT * FROM Person;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Deletes duplicate records from the Person table by keeping only the smallest id for each unique email address using a NOT IN subquery.

lessons.title: Rising Temperature
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to find all dates' `id` with higher temperatures compared to its previous dates (yesterday).

Return the result table in any order.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT w1.id FROM Weather w1 JOIN Weather w2 ON w1.recordDate = date(w2.recordDate, '+1 day') WHERE w1.temperature > w2.temperature;
lessons.defaultCode:
-- Write your SELECT query below
-- (Tip: In SQLite, compare dates using date(w2.recordDate, '+1 day') to find consecutive days)
SELECT w1.id
FROM Weather w1
JOIN Weather w2 ON 
WHERE ;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Joins the Weather table to itself comparing dates offset by 1 day using date(w2.recordDate, '+1 day'), and filters for records where the temperature is higher than the previous day.

lessons.title: Sales Person
lessons.description:
Write an SQL query to find the names of all the salespersons who did not have any orders related to the company with the name "RED".

Return the result table in any order.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT name FROM SalesPerson WHERE sales_id NOT IN (SELECT DISTINCT o.sales_id FROM Orders o INNER JOIN Company c ON o.com_id = c.com_id WHERE c.name = 'RED');

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Uses a subquery to select sales_ids of representatives who had orders linked to the company named 'RED', then selects the name of all salespersons whose IDs are NOT IN that list.

lessons.title: Second Highest Salary
lessons.description: Write an SQL query to find the second highest distinct salary from the `Employee` table. If there is no second highest salary, return `null`.
lessons.expectedQuery: SELECT (SELECT DISTINCT salary FROM Employee ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1) AS SecondHighestSalary;

testCases.name: Base Test

lessons.explanation: Retrieves the second highest distinct salary value using LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1 ordered by salary DESC inside a scalar subquery. If there is no second highest salary, SQL naturally evaluates the scalar subquery output as NULL.
